title: Repairing cracked render on an outside wall
subtitle: Rake out, key, and fill cracks in external sand-and-cement render
excerpt: How to diagnose cracked external render, distinguish superficial shrinkage cracks from structural movement, rake out and undercut a crack for a lasting repair, apply render-compatible filler or mortar, and finish to match the existing texture.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering syllabus.

content.text: External render cracks slopeinto two categories: shrinkage cracks (hairline, stable, no movement) and structural or moisture-related cracks (wider than 1 mm, returning after previous repair, or associated with
). Shrinkage cracks can be filled directly. Wider or recurring cracks must be raked out fully and the cause identified before filling.

Probe the crack with a filling knife. If it extends all the way through the render coat to the masonry beneath, it is a full-depth crack and needs mortar, not filler. Tap the area around the crack with a knuckle: a hollow sound indicates that the render has debonded from the wall and will eventually slopeaway. Any debonded area larger than 300 mm (12 in) square should be cut out and replastered rather than patched over.
Open the crack to a minimum width of 5 mm using a cold chisel or a grinder with a crack-chasing disc.

the sides of the crack so the slot is wider at the base than at the face. Remove all loose and friable material and brush out dust with a stiff brush. Dampen the inside of the crack with clean water before applying filler; dry render pulls moisture out of the repair too fast.
Step 3, choose and mix the filler

For cracks shallower than 10 mm (3/8 in) in modern sand-and-cement render: use a ready-mixed exterior filler. For cracks deeper than 10 mm or where the crack goes through to masonry: use a 3:1 sharp sand and cement mortar, or a proprietary exterior repair mortar. Do not use interior lightweight filler on external render; it absorbs water and breaks down within one winter. For movement cracks (at lintel edges, window frames, or render joints), use a flexible exterior filler or a backer rod and gun-applied mastic.

Press the filler firmly into the crack in layers no deeper than 10 mm (3/8 in) at a time; deep single fills shrink and crack. Allow each layer to firm before applying the next. Leave the final fill 1 to 2 mm below the face and add a finishing skim when the previous layer has cured. On textured surfaces, match the finish by stippling the wet filler with a sponge, brush, or the same aggregate used in the original render.
Step 5, allow to cure and paint

Allow mortar repairs to cure for at least 2 weeks before painting. Apply a masonry stabiliser to the repair area before the top coat if the surrounding wall has been previously painted. Use an exterior masonry paint and apply two coats; a single coat over a repair is obvious because the paint absorbs differently into new and old render.

attrs.fix: Rake out again to full depth and fill with a flexible exterior mastic or gun-applied polyurethane filler. These materials move with the substrate.
attrs.cause: The crack is a movement crack at a lintel or frame edge, not a shrinkage crack. Rigid filler cannot accommodate the movement.
attrs.symptom: Crack returns within the first year

attrs.fix: Break out the fill, dampen the crack well, and re-fill, pressing firmly into the full depth. Ensure each layer cures before adding the next.
attrs.cause: The crack was not dampened before filling, or the filler shrank away from the wall face as it cured.
attrs.symptom: Filled area sounds hollow when tapped after curing

attrs.fix: Apply masonry stabiliser over the repair area only, allow to dry, then apply two full coats of masonry paint over the whole section of wall rather than just the repair.
attrs.cause: New render and old render absorb paint at different rates; the repair area shows as a different sheen.
attrs.symptom: Repair is visible as a patch after painting
